ctx.name: Sam
ctx.notes: Sam is in week three and has started picking up real tickets, but he still hesitates before shipping and asks for reassurance late. I need to understand whether this is a normal confidence dip or a tooling and expectation gap we can fix quickly. I also want clarity on where onboarding handoffs are still fuzzy so he is not quietly compensating. This should stay practical and supportive: map what is unclear, unblock him, and line up the right support touchpoints for the next two weeks.

focusPoints.label: Shipping confidence and commitment consistency
focusPoints.reason: Sam has started picking up real tickets, but hesitates before shipping and asks for reassurance late. One possibility is a normal confidence dip in week three; another is a tooling or expectation gap—we should clarify which by looking at what specifically feels uncertain right before shipping.

focusPoints.label: What 'done' means for his first tickets
focusPoints.reason: Whether the bar for 'good' and the definition of done are explicit enough for a Junior in their first month. When expectations are fuzzy, people often compensate by waiting for reassurance instead of shipping with a clear checklist of what matters.

focusPoints.label: Onboarding handoffs that feel fuzzy
focusPoints.reason: The notes call out fuzzy onboarding handoffs that could be causing Sam to compensate quietly. Worth mapping the exact handoff points (who owns what, where docs live, what decisions are expected from him) so you can unblock the next two weeks with concrete ownership and artifacts.

focusPoints.label: Support touchpoints for the next two weeks
focusPoints.reason: What he would want more of from you right now—likely lightweight reassurance loops, fast review windows, and clear escalation paths—so he can build confidence without stalling. For a Junior onboarding, setting these touchpoints early prevents late reassurance from becoming the default.

transcript.question: What’s been most on your mind at work lately, Sam?
transcript.answer: I can do the tasks, but I still second-guess whether my approach matches team expectations.

transcript.question: When you say 'team expectations,' what part feels hardest to read?
transcript.answer: Local setup is better now, but I still lose time when the design and API details do not match.

transcript.question: When design and API details do not match, what is usually unclear first?
transcript.answer: I am not always sure who should approve what before I push.

transcript.question: What does “done” mean to you on your current tickets?
transcript.answer: I usually wait too long before asking because I want to solve things myself first.

transcript.question: When you wait too long to ask, what are you usually waiting to know first?
transcript.answer: When feedback is specific and fast, I move much quicker.

transcript.question: feedback specific fast, — can you say more about what that means for you right now?
transcript.answer: I still feel fuzzy on where quality bars differ between quick fixes and core flows.

transcript.question: Where do the handoffs still feel fuzzy?
transcript.answer: Pairing once or twice a week would probably prevent me from getting stuck silently.

transcript.question: What kind of check-in from me would help you most over the next two weeks?
transcript.answer: If we define what good looks like for my next two tickets, I will feel much steadier.

transcript.question: What would make the next two weeks feel steadier for you?
transcript.answer: I want to start by pairing on my next ticket so I can see the approval bar in practice.
